title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: core buzzr functionality wrapped single function, buzzr::bin_directory. function reads buzzdetect results folder (recursively), translates file times (audio timestamps seconds) date-times (e.g. “January 1, 2000 12:42 PM”), applies thresholds turn neuron activations detections, bins detections according start time. ’s example:
code: df <- buzzr::bin_directory(   dir_results=system.file('extdata/five_flowers', package='buzzr'),   thresholds = c(ins_buzz=-1.2),   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   dir_nesting = c('flower','recorder'),   tz = 'America/New_York',   binwidth = 20 ) ## Grouping time bins using columns: flower, recorder head(df) ##     flower recorder        bin_datetime detections_ins_buzz frames ##     <char>   <char>              <POSc>               <int>  <num> ## 1: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:00:00                   1   1250 ## 2: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:20:00                   4   1250 ## 3: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:40:00                   3   1250 ## 4: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:00:00                   2   1250 ## 5: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:20:00                   0   1250 ## 6: chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:40:00                   0   1250

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: walkthrough, use buzzdetect results included package. results correspond recordings described buzzdetect manuscript. Recorders placed five flowers bloom (chicory, mustard, pumpkin, soybean, watermelon) allowed record 24 hours. resulting audio analyzed buzzdetect; ’ll refer resulting dataset “Five Flowers” dataset. keep buzzr version dataset light, retained single recorder plant trimmed files. Find full dataset Zenodo repository.

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: path Five Flowers dataset machine can retrieved system.file function. Let’s look. see results stored folders according pattern flower/recorder_id/HHMMDD_MMSS_buzzdetect.csv.
code: library(buzzr) dir_data <- system.file('extdata/five_flowers', package='buzzr')  paths_results <- list.files(   dir_data,   recursive=T )  head(paths_results) ## [1] "chicory/1_104/250704_0000_buzzdetect.csv"   ## [2] "mustard/54/240904_0000_buzzdetect.csv"      ## [3] "pumpkin/1_37/240808_0000_buzzdetect.csv"    ## [4] "soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv"       ## [5] "watermelon/1_73/240727_0000_buzzdetect.csv"

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: Let’s look single results file using base R: start column activations different neurons. row single frame, 0.96s span audio results.
code: path_results <- file.path(dir_data, paths_results[1])  df_base <- read.csv(path_results) head(df_base) ##   start activation_ins_buzz activation_ambient_rain activation_ins_trill ## 1  0.00                -1.7                    -2.0                 -1.9 ## 2  0.96                -2.0                    -2.0                 -1.9 ## 3  1.92                -2.1                    -2.8                 -2.8 ## 4  2.88                -2.0                    -2.6                 -2.4 ## 5  3.84                -2.0                    -2.3                 -2.4 ## 6  4.80                -1.8                    -1.5                 -1.6 ##   activation_mech_plane ## 1                  -2.2 ## 2                  -2.4 ## 3                  -3.4 ## 4                  -3.2 ## 5                  -2.5 ## 6                  -2.3

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: Result files can well hundred thousand rows long, read.csv quite slow. Let’s try buzzr instead, leverages data.table::fread. function want buzzr::read_results can see buzzr renamed default “start” column buzzr convention “start_filetime”. allows buzzr distinguish timestamps file-time (seconds start file) timestamps real-world time (date-time).
code: df_buzzr <- buzzr::read_results(path_results) head(df_buzzr) ##    start_filetime activation_ins_buzz activation_ambient_rain ##             <num>               <num>                   <num> ## 1:           0.00                -1.7                    -2.0 ## 2:           0.96                -2.0                    -2.0 ## 3:           1.92                -2.1                    -2.8 ## 4:           2.88                -2.0                    -2.6 ## 5:           3.84                -2.0                    -2.3 ## 6:           4.80                -1.8                    -1.5 ##    activation_ins_trill activation_mech_plane ##                   <num>                 <num> ## 1:                 -1.9                  -2.2 ## 2:                 -1.9                  -2.4 ## 3:                 -2.8                  -3.4 ## 4:                 -2.4                  -3.2 ## 5:                 -2.4                  -2.5 ## 6:                 -1.6                  -2.3

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: can use buzzr interpret date-time recording name results file. , need several arguments. posix_formats: string (vector strings) containing POSIX format code. files saved 2-digit year, 2-digit month, 2-digit day, underscore, 24-hour hour, minute. , “YYMMDD_HHMM”. way write POSIX format string : %y%m%d_%H%M. first_match: multiple POSIX format strings supplied file matches multiple formats, first match used (TRUE) NA returned (FALSE, default)? won’t needed ’re working single file, multiple format (perhaps using different recorders experiment), allows read_directory bin_directory read results one go. drop_filetime: output results retain start_filetime column alongside start_datetime (FALSE) dropped (TRUE)? Note: argument respected POSIX formats given. File-time dropped time column. tz: time zone use POSIX translation. Required translating results date-time. match time zone recordings. Time zones real nightmare. ’re finding everything plotting incorrectly, ’s probably time zone issue. Date-time data unfortunately cumbersome, figure settings per project. Now can see ’ve successfully translated file-time date-time. “start_filetime” present (since chose drop ), also “start_datetime” holding date-time. Usually care date-time, file-time can useful. example, find frames detected buzzes use FFmpeg extract audio corresponding file times.
code: df_datetime <- buzzr::read_results(   path_results,   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',  # YYMMDD_HHMM   first_match = FALSE,  # doesn't matter in this case, since we have only one format   drop_filetime = FALSE,  # keep the start_filetime column (by default, it's dropped)   tz='America/New_York'  # the time zone these data were collected in )  head(df_datetime) ##         start_datetime start_filetime activation_ins_buzz ##                 <POSc>          <num>               <num> ## 1: 2025-07-04 00:00:00           0.00                -1.7 ## 2: 2025-07-04 00:00:00           0.96                -2.0 ## 3: 2025-07-04 00:00:01           1.92                -2.1 ## 4: 2025-07-04 00:00:02           2.88                -2.0 ## 5: 2025-07-04 00:00:03           3.84                -2.0 ## 6: 2025-07-04 00:00:04           4.80                -1.8 ##    activation_ambient_rain activation_ins_trill activation_mech_plane ##                      <num>                <num>                 <num> ## 1:                    -2.0                 -1.9                  -2.2 ## 2:                    -2.0                 -1.9                  -2.4 ## 3:                    -2.8                 -2.8                  -3.4 ## 4:                    -2.6                 -2.4                  -3.2 ## 5:                    -2.3                 -2.4                  -2.5 ## 6:                    -1.5                 -1.6                  -2.3

what: Using buzzr to interpret file structure
title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: ’ll probably find convenient organize recordings according schema. example, dataset placed recordings folders corresponding recorder ID placed folders ones corresponding plant type. buzzr lets us extract information file path. can two arguments: dir_nesting: character vector used name directory levels results file. , level results represent? element becomes column output, storing components path data file. return_ident: available read_directory bin_directory, file identifier placed column? ident relative path results directory file, stripped extension _buzzdetect tag. probably won’t care unless want pin specific problematic recording. example, see large spike positives 3:00AM, may want investigate. isn’t terribly useful ’re dealing single file, ’s convenient read entire directory. However, file structure must rectangular! , every file level nesting. foo/bar/results.csv foo/bar/lorem/results.csv won’t work.
code: df_nesting <- buzzr::read_results(   path_results,   dir_nesting = c('flower', 'recorder') )  head(df_nesting) ##     flower recorder start_filetime activation_ins_buzz activation_ambient_rain ##     <char>   <char>          <num>               <num>                   <num> ## 1: chicory    1_104           0.00                -1.7                    -2.0 ## 2: chicory    1_104           0.96                -2.0                    -2.0 ## 3: chicory    1_104           1.92                -2.1                    -2.8 ## 4: chicory    1_104           2.88                -2.0                    -2.6 ## 5: chicory    1_104           3.84                -2.0                    -2.3 ## 6: chicory    1_104           4.80                -1.8                    -1.5 ##    activation_ins_trill activation_mech_plane ##                   <num>                 <num> ## 1:                 -1.9                  -2.2 ## 2:                 -1.9                  -2.4 ## 3:                 -2.8                  -3.4 ## 4:                 -2.4                  -3.2 ## 5:                 -2.4                  -2.5 ## 6:                 -1.6                  -2.3

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: Raw neuron activations useful ; threshold must applied convert activations discrete detections. threshold call detections depends model ’re using want balance detection sensitivity false positive rate. recommend using threshold 95% precision (95% called buzzes true) starting point, may want fine-tune detections low sources false positives audio. model_general_v3, threshold -1.2 good starting point calling buzzes. threshold roughly correspond 95% precision. Activations value count detected instances buzzing. vignette, ’ll also try detecting planes threshold -2. threshold arbitrary untested, let us see buzzr handles multiple detection columns. can call detections applying function buzzr::call_detections results data frame neuron activations. call_detections takes one argument addition results data frame: thresholds: named numeric vector names corresponding neurons binned values corresponding desired threshold. can see retain two time columns, “activation_” columns named thresholds replaced “detections_”. Neurons without threshold dropped. detections binary TRUE/FALSE values. may interested total number detections, case simply sum column. Across whole day, saw 2,604 detections 90,000 frames average detection rate 0.03. total detection rates valuable cases, common interest change detection rate time. , need bin.
code: df_called <- buzzr::call_detections(   df_datetime,   thresholds = c(     ins_buzz = -1.2,     mech_plane = -2   ) )  head(df_called) ##         start_datetime start_filetime detections_ins_buzz detections_mech_plane ##                 <POSc>          <num>              <lgcl>                <lgcl> ## 1: 2025-07-04 00:00:00           0.00               FALSE                 FALSE ## 2: 2025-07-04 00:00:00           0.96               FALSE                 FALSE ## 3: 2025-07-04 00:00:01           1.92               FALSE                 FALSE ## 4: 2025-07-04 00:00:02           2.88               FALSE                 FALSE ## 5: 2025-07-04 00:00:03           3.84               FALSE                 FALSE ## 6: 2025-07-04 00:00:04           4.80               FALSE                 FALSE total_detections <- sum(df_called$detections_ins_buzz) print(total_detections) ## [1] 2604

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: bin detections time, can apply function buzzr::bin results data frame called detections. bin needs two arguments addition data frame: binwidth: size bin minutes calculate_rate: want buzzr automatically calculate detection rates (detections/frames) detection columns? might seem like always desired, sometimes want modify binned results multiple times. E.g., first bin width 1 compress data, bin width 15 graphing width 60 statistical analysis. case, might well save rate calculation end. can see buzzr handled file-time date-time problem now detections turned booleans integers. Let’s see data look like!  Cool! see obvious trend buzz detections launching early morning, dropping midday. false positives night, ’re dwarfed signal daytime.

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: binning, buzzr drops columns frame times, neuron activations, detection rates. , bin, frame counts total detections summed. requested, detection rates also calculated. Every remaining column used group binning. cause unexpected results ’ve created custom columns. example: buzzr prints message showing grouping columns just case groupings unintended. can see bins contain half many frames expected weren’t aware grouping logic.
code: df_called$newcol <- rep(c('A', 'B'), nrow(df_called)/2)  df_bin_customgroups <- buzzr::bin(   df_called,   binwidth=15,  # 15-minute bins   calculate_rate = TRUE  # calculate our detection rates for us ) ## Grouping time bins using columns: newcol head(df_bin_customgroups) ##    newcol bin_filetime        bin_datetime detections_ins_buzz ##    <char>        <num>              <POSc>               <int> ## 1:      A            0 2025-07-04 00:00:00                   1 ## 2:      B            0 2025-07-04 00:00:00                   0 ## 3:      A          900 2025-07-04 00:15:00                   1 ## 4:      B          900 2025-07-04 00:15:00                   2 ## 5:      B         1800 2025-07-04 00:30:00                   2 ## 6:      A         1800 2025-07-04 00:30:00                   0 ##    detections_mech_plane frames detectionrate_ins_buzz detectionrate_mech_plane ##                    <int>  <num>                  <num>                    <num> ## 1:                    16    469            0.002132196               0.03411514 ## 2:                    21    469            0.000000000               0.04477612 ## 3:                    11    469            0.002132196               0.02345416 ## 4:                    11    468            0.004273504               0.02350427 ## 5:                    56    469            0.004264392               0.11940299 ## 6:                    50    469            0.000000000               0.10660981

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: steps can completed every file directory (subdirectories) single command: buzzr::bin_directory. , just combine arguments ’ve seen . Rebinning requires single command!
code: df_fullbin <- buzzr::bin_directory(   dir_results=dir_data,   thresholds = c(ins_buzz=-1.2),   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   first_match = FALSE,   drop_filetime = TRUE,   dir_nesting = c('flower','recorder'),   return_ident = TRUE,   tz = 'America/New_York',   binwidth = 20,   calculate_rate = TRUE ) ## Grouping time bins using columns: flower, recorder head(df_fullbin) ##                        ident  flower recorder        bin_datetime ##                       <char>  <char>   <char>              <POSc> ## 1: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:00:00 ## 2: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:20:00 ## 3: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:40:00 ## 4: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:00:00 ## 5: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:20:00 ## 6: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:40:00 ##    detections_ins_buzz frames detectionrate_ins_buzz ##                  <int>  <num>                  <num> ## 1:                   1   1250                 0.0008 ## 2:                   4   1250                 0.0032 ## 3:                   3   1250                 0.0024 ## 4:                   2   1250                 0.0016 ## 5:                   0   1250                 0.0000 ## 6:                   0   1250                 0.0000 print(unique(df_fullbin$flower)) ## [1] "chicory"    "mustard"    "pumpkin"    "soybean"    "watermelon" head(bin(df_fullbin, 120)) ## Grouping time bins using columns: flower, recorder ##                        ident  flower recorder        bin_datetime ##                       <char>  <char>   <char>              <POSc> ## 1: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:00:00 ## 2: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 02:00:00 ## 3: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 04:00:00 ## 4: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 06:00:00 ## 5: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 08:00:00 ## 6: chicory/1_104/250704_0000 chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 10:00:00 ##    detections_ins_buzz frames ##                  <int>  <num> ## 1:                  10   7500 ## 2:                   7   7500 ## 3:                   1   7500 ## 4:                 878   7500 ## 5:                1460   7500 ## 6:                 111   7500

title: Using buzzr to handle buzzdetect data
text: may also want read directory without binning results. example may want try multiple different thresholds efficiently, maybe want extract positive frames, examine behavior activation values. function buzzr::read_directory, behaves expect given information .
code: df_fullread <- buzzr::read_directory(   dir_results=dir_data,   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   first_match = FALSE,   drop_filetime = TRUE,   dir_nesting = c('flower','recorder'),   return_ident = TRUE,   tz = 'America/New_York' )  nrow(df_fullread) ## [1] 450000

what: Reading a timestamp from a file name
title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: field recorders embed recording start time file name. buzzr reads using POSIX format codes base R’s strptime(). Common examples: Five Flowers dataset uses YYMMDD_HHMM pattern. Let’s parse single file: _buzzdetect suffix file extension stripped matching, format string needs describe timestamp part name. can also call file_start_time() directly just want recording’s start time without reading full file: works vector paths :

title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: Always pass tz argument. omit , R uses system time zone, silently produce wrong results analyse data collected different zone. Use OlsonNames() browse valid time zone strings.
code: # Same file, two different time zones — note the hour difference file_start_time(path, posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M', tz = 'America/New_York') #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT" file_start_time(path, posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M', tz = 'America/Chicago') #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 CDT"

title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: default, start_filetime dropped start_datetime added. Set drop_filetime = FALSE keep : File-time useful want extract audio clips later (e.g. using FFmpeg cut specific second source file).
code: head(   read_results(     path,     posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',     tz = 'America/New_York',     drop_filetime = FALSE   ),   3 ) #>         start_datetime start_filetime activation_ins_trill #>                 <POSc>          <num>                <num> #> 1: 2023-08-09 06:00:00           0.00                 -2.8 #> 2: 2023-08-09 06:00:00           0.96                 -2.5 #> 3: 2023-08-09 06:00:01           1.92                 -2.2 #>    activation_mech_plane activation_ambient_rain activation_ins_buzz #>                    <num>                   <num>               <num> #> 1:                  -2.3                    -2.4                -2.2 #> 2:                  -2.7                    -2.5                -2.3 #> 3:                  -2.4                    -2.4                -2.4

what: Mixed recorder types: multiple format strings
title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: experiment used one recorder model, file names may follow different timestamp conventions. Supply formats character vector: file matches exactly one format, everything resolves cleanly:
code: # Imagine two recorder types in the same experiment: #   AudioMoth:  YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS  e.g. 20230809_060000_buzzdetect.csv #   SongMeter:  YYMMDD_HHMM      e.g. 230809_0600_buzzdetect.csv  paths_mixed <- c(   'site_a/audiomoth/20230809_060000_buzzdetect.csv',  # AudioMoth   'site_b/songmeter/230809_0600_buzzdetect.csv'       # SongMeter )  formats_mixed <- c('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S', '%y%m%d_%H%M') file_start_time(paths_mixed, posix_formats = formats_mixed, tz = 'America/New_York') #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT" "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT"

previous_headings: Mixed recorder types: multiple format strings
what: When a file matches two formats with different results
title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: things get tricky. SongMeter file like 230809_0600 match %y%m%d_%H%M (→ 2023) can also match %Y%m%d_%H%M%S interpreted year 0230 (implausible date, R won’t reject ). dangerously, 20230809_060000 matches %Y%m%d_%H%M%S (→ 2023-08-09 06:00:00) also %y%m%d_%H%M (→ year 2020, completely wrong). default (first_match = FALSE), buzzr returns NA warns formats conflict: know format take priority, set first_match = TRUE accept first matching format move : order posix_formats matters first_match = TRUE. Put specific (trusted) format first.
code: # Construct a path that matches both formats ambiguously ambiguous_path <- 'site/recorder/20230809_060000_buzzdetect.csv'  file_start_time(   ambiguous_path,   posix_formats = c('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S', '%y%m%d_%H%M'),   tz = 'America/New_York',   first_match = FALSE ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT" # With '%Y%m%d_%H%M%S' listed first, the four-digit-year parse wins file_start_time(   ambiguous_path,   posix_formats = c('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S', '%y%m%d_%H%M'),   tz = 'America/New_York',   first_match = TRUE ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT"  # Swap the order — now the two-digit-year parse wins (wrong!) file_start_time(   ambiguous_path,   posix_formats = c('%y%m%d_%H%M', '%Y%m%d_%H%M%S'),   tz = 'America/New_York',   first_match = TRUE ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT"

previous_headings: Mixed recorder types: multiple format strings
what: When a file matches two formats with the same result
title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: two formats resolve identical timestamps, buzzr silently deduplicates — warning, NA:
code: # These two formats both parse '230809_0600' as 2023-08-09 06:00:00 # (one by coincidence of the numeric overlap) file_start_time(   'site/recorder/230809_0600_buzzdetect.csv',   posix_formats = c('%y%m%d_%H%M', '%y%m%d_%H%M'),  # duplicate format   tz = 'America/New_York' ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT"

previous_headings: Mixed recorder types: multiple format strings
title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: buzzr returns NA warning, downstream code doesn’t fail silently:
code: file_start_time(   'site/recorder/no_timestamp_here_buzzdetect.csv',   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   tz = 'America/New_York' ) #> Warning: Returning NA for start time of file #> 'site/recorder/no_timestamp_here_buzzdetect.csv'. No POSIX matches found. #> [1] NA

title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: recordings span multiple calendar days can’t overlay directly datetime axis — dates differ even time day . commontime() solves setting every date arbitrary day (2000-01-01) preserving time day: Now five flowers share x axis, regardless day recorded:  Always pass tz commontime() label_hour(). differ, axis labels won’t match data.
code: dir <- system.file('extdata/five_flowers_snip', package = 'buzzr')  binned <- bin_directory(   dir_results   = dir,   thresholds    = c(ins_buzz = -1.2),   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   tz            = 'America/New_York',   dir_nesting   = c('flower', 'recorder'),   binwidth       = 20,   calculate_rate = TRUE ) #> Grouping time bins using columns: flower, recorder  # Dates vary across the five recordings; commontime collapses them range(binned$bin_datetime) #> [1] "2023-08-09 06:00:00 EDT" "2025-07-04 20:00:00 EDT"  binned$time_of_day <- commontime(binned$bin_datetime, tz = 'America/New_York') range(binned$time_of_day) #> [1] "2000-01-01 06:00:00 EST" "2000-01-01 20:00:00 EST" library(ggplot2)  ggplot(binned, aes(x = time_of_day, y = detectionrate_ins_buzz, color = flower)) +   geom_line() +   scale_x_datetime(labels = label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York')) +   labs(x = 'Time of day', y = 'Detection\nrate', color = 'Flower') +   theme_buzzr()

title: Interpreting time in buzzr
text: statistical modelling — circular regression, GAMs, peak-time estimates — ’s convenient time number. time_of_day() returns either proportion 24-hour day decimal hour: Unlike commontime(), strips date entirely returns plain number, easier pass modelling functions:
code: times <- as.POSIXct(   c('2023-08-09 00:00:00', '2023-08-09 06:00:00',     '2023-08-09 12:00:00', '2023-08-09 18:30:00'),   tz = 'America/New_York' )  # Proportion: midnight = 0, noon = 0.5, next midnight = 1 time_of_day(times) #> [1] 0.0000000 0.2500000 0.5000000 0.7708333  # Decimal hours: midnight = 0, 6 AM = 6, 6:30 PM = 18.5 time_of_day(times, time_format = 'hour') #> [1]  0.0  6.0 12.0 18.5 binned$hour <- time_of_day(binned$bin_datetime, time_format = 'hour')  # e.g. find the hour of peak detections for each flower binned[, .SD[which.max(detectionrate_ins_buzz)][, .(peak_hour = hour)],        by = flower] #>        flower peak_hour #>        <char>     <num> #> 1:    chicory         9 #> 2:    mustard        11 #> 3:    pumpkin         8 #> 4:    soybean        13 #> 5: watermelon        13

title: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results
text: buzzr R package reading, shaping, plotting results buzzdetect. See walkthrough vignette detailed introduction! buzzr yet hosted CRAN, installed usual install.packages call. Instead, install package devtools (can installed via install.packages), run command devtools::install_github(repo='OSU-Bee-Lab/buzzr').

what: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
text: Groups frame-level results fixed-width time bins sums detections_ columns frames column within bin. remaining columns (e.g. dir_nesting) used grouping variables, bins computed separately unique combination columns.

title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
code: bin(results, binwidth, calculate_rate = F)

title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
text: results data frame data.table buzzdetect results, created read_results.. Must contain least one time column (start_filetime, start_datetime, bin_filetime, bin_datetime) least one detections_ column. binwidth Width time bin minutes (e.g. 5, 20, 60). calculate_rate TRUE, adds detectionrate_ column detections_ column, calculated detections divided frames. Values range 0 1.

title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
text: data.table bin_filetime bin_datetime column, summed detections_ columns, frames column, optionally detectionrate_ columns. input columns retained grouping variables.

title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin
text: Can also re-bin previously binned results. cleanest results, choose new binwidth multiple original (e.g. re-bin 5-minute bins 60-minute bins). Non-multiple re-binning allowed produces boundary artefacts. Detection rates (detectionrate_ columns) best calculated final binning step. plan re-bin, leave calculate_rate = FALSE .

title: Bin results by time, summing detections and frames per bin. — bin

what: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
text: convenience wrapper runs read_directory, call_detections, bin sequence. arguments functions accepted . See documentation details.

title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
code: bin_directory(   dir_results,   thresholds,   posix_formats = NA,   first_match = FALSE,   drop_filetime = TRUE,   dir_nesting = NULL,   return_ident = FALSE,   tz = NA,   binwidth = 5,   calculate_rate = FALSE )

title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
text: dir_results directory holding buzzdetect results read. thresholds named numeric vector mapping neuron names detection thresholds (e.g. c(ins_buzz = -1.2)). Frames whose activation value exceeds threshold counted detections. model_general_v3 outputs negative log-likelihoods, thresholds typically negative. posix_formats Character vector POSIX format strings (see base::strptime) describing timestamp embedded file name (e.g. '%y%m%d_%H%M' 230809_0600). Supply multiple strings recordings different logger types mixed one directory. NA (default), results left file-time. See file_start_time. first_match Controls behaviour multiple formats produce different times file. FALSE (default) returns NA warning; TRUE accepts time first matching format message. drop_filetime TRUE (default), start_filetime / bin_filetime column removed start_datetime / bin_datetime added. Set FALSE keep . Ignored POSIX formats given. dir_nesting character vector used name directory levels results file. element becomes column output, storing components path. example, dir_nesting = c('site', 'recorder') path data/2026/siteA/recorder_4/250704_11343_buzzdetect.csv add site column (holding value 'siteA') recorder column (holding value 'recorder_4'). return_ident 'ident' relative path data directory results file, without _buzzdetect tag file extension. Useful finding corresponding audio files annotations. Set TRUE add ident column first column output. tz Time zone string passed base::.POSIXct (e.g. 'America/New_York'). See OlsonNames() valid values.. Ignored results already contain start_datetime bin_datetime column. binwidth Width time bin minutes (e.g. 5, 20, 60). calculate_rate TRUE, adds detectionrate_ column detections_ column, calculated detections divided frames. Values range 0 1.

title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
text: data.table bin_filetime bin_datetime, detections_ columns, frames column, columns dir_nesting ident.

title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
text: recommended entry point analyses: point results folder, supply thresholds time zone, get back tidy binned data.table ready plotting modelling.

title: Read, threshold, and bin all buzzdetect result files in a directory. — bin_directory
code: dir <- system.file('extdata/five_flowers', package = 'buzzr')  bin_directory(   dir_results    = dir,   thresholds     = c(ins_buzz = -1.2),   posix_formats  = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   tz             = 'America/New_York',   dir_nesting    = c('flower', 'recorder'),   binwidth       = 20,   calculate_rate = TRUE ) #> Grouping time bins using columns: flower, recorder #>          flower recorder        bin_datetime detections_ins_buzz frames #>          <char>   <char>              <POSc>               <int>  <num> #>   1:    chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:00:00                   1   1250 #>   2:    chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:20:00                   4   1250 #>   3:    chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 00:40:00                   3   1250 #>   4:    chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:00:00                   2   1250 #>   5:    chicory    1_104 2025-07-04 01:20:00                   0   1250 #>  ---                                                                    #> 356: watermelon     1_73 2024-07-27 22:20:00                   1   1250 #> 357: watermelon     1_73 2024-07-27 22:40:00                   0   1250 #> 358: watermelon     1_73 2024-07-27 23:00:00                   2   1250 #> 359: watermelon     1_73 2024-07-27 23:20:00                   1   1250 #> 360: watermelon     1_73 2024-07-27 23:40:00                   0   1250 #>      detectionrate_ins_buzz #>                       <num> #>   1:                 0.0008 #>   2:                 0.0032 #>   3:                 0.0024 #>   4:                 0.0016 #>   5:                 0.0000 #>  ---                        #> 356:                 0.0008 #> 357:                 0.0000 #> 358:                 0.0016 #> 359:                 0.0008 #> 360:                 0.0000

what: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
title: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
text: buzzr provides pipeline turning raw buzzdetect results plotable, modelable, useful results.

title: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
text: Run bin_directory automate steps .

title: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
text: step--step workflow can nice still working pipeline. example, maybe want tune threshold, want bin_directory re-read results every time. First read_directory, try bunch different thresholds call_detections. 1. Read results Point read_directory output folder buzzdetect. Optionally, let buzzr convert file times real-world date times supplying POSIX format string (e.g. '%y%m%d_%H%M') time zone. Tell buzzr understand data organization turning path levels (e.g. ./year/site/recorder/foo_buzzdetect.csv) columns. 2. Call detections Pass raw activation values call_detections named threshold vector (e.g. c(ins_buzz = -1.2)). produces detections_ column named neuron, value TRUE activation exceeds threshold frame. 3. Bin time Bin frame-level detections fixed-width time bins bin Set calculate_rate = TRUE add detectionrate_ column (detections / frames). 4. Plot Use commontime collapse recordings different dates onto shared time--day axis, label_hour format x-axis, theme_buzzr clean ggplot2 theme. complete worked example see vignette('buzzr').

title: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
text: bin_directory One-stop-shop. Read, threshold, bin entire results folder one call. read_results Read single buzzdetect CSV RDS result file. call_detections Apply activation thresholds produce binary detections. bin Aggregate frame-level results time bins. commontime Collapse dates onto shared time--day axis plotting. theme_buzzr ggplot2 theme styled buzzdetect result plots.

title: buzzr: Tools for wrangling buzzdetect results — buzzr-package
text: trim_directory Dataset big? Drop unnecessary detail save compressed RDS objects (faster read, !)

what: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
text: Converts raw neuron activation values binary detections applying numeric threshold named neuron. Frames activation exceeds threshold marked TRUE; others FALSE. activation_ columns dropped output — use bin afterwards summarise detection counts time.

title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
code: call_detections(results, thresholds)

title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
text: results data frame data.table buzzdetect results, created read_results.. Must contain activation_ columns neuron named thresholds. thresholds named numeric vector mapping neuron names detection thresholds (e.g. c(ins_buzz = -1.2)). Frames whose activation value exceeds threshold counted detections. model_general_v3 outputs negative log-likelihoods, thresholds typically negative. Activations threshold value counted detections, thresholds models output negative log-likelihoods (model_general_v3) typically negative (e.g. -1.2).

title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
text: data.table detections_ columns replacing activation_ columns. detection column contains logical (TRUE/FALSE) values.

title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
text: original data frame never modified; copy returned.

title: Call event detections using activation thresholds. — call_detections
code: path <- system.file(   'extdata/five_flowers/soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv',   package = 'buzzr' ) results <- read_results(path)  # Single neuron: activations above -1.2 are counted as buzz detections call_detections(results, thresholds = c(ins_buzz = -1.2)) #>        start_filetime detections_ins_buzz #>                 <num>              <lgcl> #>     1:           0.00               FALSE #>     2:           0.96               FALSE #>     3:           1.92               FALSE #>     4:           2.88               FALSE #>     5:           3.84               FALSE #>    ---                                    #> 89996:       86395.20               FALSE #> 89997:       86396.16               FALSE #> 89998:       86397.12               FALSE #> 89999:       86398.08               FALSE #> 90000:       86399.04               FALSE  # Multiple neurons at once call_detections(results, thresholds = c(ins_buzz = -1.2, mech_plane = -2.0)) #>        start_filetime detections_ins_buzz detections_mech_plane #>                 <num>              <lgcl>                <lgcl> #>     1:           0.00               FALSE                  TRUE #>     2:           0.96               FALSE                 FALSE #>     3:           1.92               FALSE                 FALSE #>     4:           2.88               FALSE                 FALSE #>     5:           3.84               FALSE                 FALSE #>    ---                                                          #> 89996:       86395.20               FALSE                 FALSE #> 89997:       86396.16               FALSE                 FALSE #> 89998:       86397.12               FALSE                 FALSE #> 89999:       86398.08               FALSE                 FALSE #> 90000:       86399.04               FALSE                 FALSE

what: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
text: Sets date every element times 2000-01-01 keeping time--day unchanged. lets overlay recordings different calendar days shared x axis using ggplot2::scale_x_datetime label_hour. arbitrary date 2000-01-01 chosen far enough DST transition dates avoid edge cases time zones.

title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
code: commontime(times, tz = Sys.timezone())

title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
text: times POSIXct vector. tz Time zone string (e.g. 'America/New_York'). Defaults system time zone. match tz argument passed label_hour.

title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
text: POSIXct vector length times, date component set 2000-01-01.

title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
text: Time zone tip: pass tz pass label_hour ensure hour labels match data.

title: Coerce all dates to the same day, preserving time of day. — commontime
code: times <- as.POSIXct(   c('2023-08-09 06:15:00', '2024-07-27 14:30:00'),   tz = 'America/New_York' )  # Both dates become 2000-01-01; times of day are preserved commontime(times, tz = 'America/New_York') #> [1] "2000-01-01 06:15:00 EST" "2000-01-01 14:30:00 EST"  # Typical ggplot2 usage (not run here): # ggplot(df, aes(x = commontime(bin_datetime, tz = 'America/New_York'), #               y = detectionrate_ins_buzz, color = flower)) + #   geom_line() + #   scale_x_datetime(labels = label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York'))

what: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
title: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
text: Evaluate performance buzzdetect analysis log. Reports: duration_total - many seconds analysis take? duration_audio - many seconds audio analyzed? analysis_rate - many seconds audio analyzed per second wall time? NOTE! use duration_total divisor, starts counting first chunk reported. bottleneck_time - long analyzers wait streamers fill queue? duration_warmup - long launching analysis first chunk analyzed? Includes results checking cleaning, streamer launching, etc.

title: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
code: evaluate_log(path_log)

title: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
text: path_log String. path buzzdetect results log.

title: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
text: named vector analysis metrics

title: Summarize a buzzdetect log file — evaluate_log
text: Useful tuning analysis settings.

what: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
text: Extract recording start date-times file names.

title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
code: file_start_time(paths, posix_formats, tz, first_match = FALSE)

title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
text: paths Character vector file paths. posix_formats Character vector POSIX format strings (see base::strptime) describing timestamp embedded file name (e.g. '%y%m%d_%H%M' 230809_0600). Supply multiple strings recordings different logger types mixed one directory. See base::strptime format codes. tz Time zone string passed base::.POSIXct (e.g. 'America/New_York'). See OlsonNames() valid values. first_match Controls behaviour multiple formats produce different times file. FALSE (default) returns NA warning; TRUE accepts time first matching format message.

title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
text: POSIXct vector length paths.

title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
text: Parses date-time information embedded file names using one POSIX format strings (see base::strptime format codes). file extension _buzzdetect suffix stripped matching, format string describe timestamp portion base name. single format unambiguously matches, parsed time returned. multiple formats match file result, duplicate silently ignored. produce different times, behaviour depends first_match: FALSE (default): returns NA warning listing conflicting formats. TRUE: returns time first matching format message. format matches, NA returned warning.

title: Extract date time information from a file's name. — file_start_time
code: # Single file, single format (YYMMDD_HHMM) file_start_time(   'soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv',   posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M',   tz = 'America/New_York' ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 EDT"  # Multiple files in one call paths <- c(   'soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv',   'chicory/1_104/250704_0000_buzzdetect.csv' ) file_start_time(paths, posix_formats = '%y%m%d_%H%M', tz = 'America/New_York') #> [1] "2023-08-09 EDT" "2025-07-04 EDT"  # Two formats for two recorder types in the same experiment; # first_match = FALSE returns NA (with a warning) if both match a file file_start_time(   paths,   posix_formats = c('%y%m%d_%H%M', '%Y%m%d_%H%M%S'),   tz = 'America/New_York',   first_match = FALSE ) #> [1] "2023-08-09 EDT" "2025-07-04 EDT"

what: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
title: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
text: convenience function checking whether binned results data.table right number frames. Frames per bin = (binwidth_min * 60) / framelength_s. Compare frames column binned result identify bins missing audio (e.g. recorder dropouts files shorter bin width).

title: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
code: frames_expected(binwidth_min, framelength_s)

title: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
text: binwidth_min Numeric. Bin width minutes. framelength_s Numeric. Frame length seconds. model_general_v3 0.96.

title: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
text: single numeric value: expected number frames per full bin.

title: Calculate the expected number of frames in a time bin. — frames_expected
code: # model_general_v3 uses 0.96-second frames frames_expected(binwidth_min = 5,  framelength_s = 0.96)  # 312.5 #> [1] 312.5 frames_expected(binwidth_min = 20, framelength_s = 0.96)  # 1250 #> [1] 1250 frames_expected(binwidth_min = 60, framelength_s = 0.96)  # 3750 #> [1] 3750  # Check for bins with incomplete audio coverage if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ binned <- bin_directory(dir, thresholds = c(ins_buzz = -1.2), binwidth = 20) expected <- frames_expected(20, 0.96) binned[frames < expected * 0.9]  # bins with > 10% missing audio } # }

what: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
text: Strips results directory prefix, _buzzdetect tag, file extension, leaving clean relative path uniquely identifies recording. ident can used locate corresponding audio files, annotation files, metadata.

title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
code: get_ident(path_in, dir_in = "")

title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
text: path_in Character vector file paths. dir_in root results directory strip front path. Defaults '' (returns full path minus extension tag).

title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
text: character vector length path_in.

title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
text: example, given dir_in = '/data/results' path_in = '/data/results/soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv', returned ident 'soybean/9/230809_0000'.

title: Extract the identifier (ident) from a buzzdetect result file path. — get_ident
code: dir  <- system.file('extdata/five_flowers', package = 'buzzr') path <- system.file(   'extdata/five_flowers/soybean/9/230809_0000_buzzdetect.csv',   package = 'buzzr' )  # Returns 'soybean/9/230809_0000' get_ident(path, dir) #> [1] "soybean/9/230809_0000"  # Works on a vector of paths all_paths <- list.files(dir, pattern = '_buzzdetect', recursive = TRUE,                         full.names = TRUE) get_ident(all_paths, dir) #> [1] "chicory/1_104/250704_0000"   "mustard/54/240904_0000"      #> [3] "pumpkin/1_37/240808_0000"    "soybean/9/230809_0000"       #> [5] "watermelon/1_73/240727_0000"

what: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
title: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
text: Returns labeller function formats POSIXct values readable hour strings (e.g. "6 ", "2 pm"). Designed use ggplot2::scale_x_datetime converting time column commontime.

title: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
text: tz Time zone string (e.g. 'America/New_York'). Defaults system time zone. match tz argument passed commontime.

title: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
text: function accepts POSIXct vector returns character vector hour labels.

title: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
text: Time zone tip: use tz passed commontime axis labels match data.

title: ggplot2 x-axis label formatter for time-of-day plots. — label_hour
code: # Typical usage with ggplot2 (not run): # ggplot(df, aes(x = commontime(bin_datetime, tz = 'America/New_York'), #               y = detectionrate_ins_buzz, color = flower)) + #   geom_line() + #   scale_x_datetime(labels = label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York')) + #   theme_buzzr()  # The labeller itself: fmt <- label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York') fmt(as.POSIXct('2000-01-01 08:00:00', tz = 'America/New_York'))  # "8 am" #> [1] "8 am" fmt(as.POSIXct('2000-01-01 14:00:00', tz = 'America/New_York'))  # "2 pm" #> [1] "2 pm"

Supply multiple strings recordings different logger types mixed one directory. NA (default), results left file-time. what: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
title: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
text: Applies clean, publication-ready style horizontal y-axis titles, border around panel, suppressed minor gridlines. Available light dark variants — dark variant uses deep purple palette plot background.

title: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
code: theme_buzzr(base_size = 10, mode = "light")

title: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
text: base_size Numeric. Base font size points. text elements scale relative value. Defaults 10. mode Character. Color scheme — 'light' (default) white background, 'dark' deep-purple background suited presentations spectrograms.

title: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
text: ggplot2::theme object can added ggplot.

title: A ggplot2 theme for aesthetic plotting of buzzdetect results. — theme_buzzr
code: # Light mode (default) — typical use in a detection-rate time-of-day plot: # ggplot(binned, aes(x = commontime(bin_datetime, tz = 'America/New_York'), #                    y = detectionrate_ins_buzz, color = flower)) + #   geom_line() + #   scale_x_datetime(labels = label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York')) + #   labs(x = 'Time of day', y = 'Detection\nrate') + #   theme_buzzr()  # Dark mode — useful for presentations or pairing with spectrograms: # ggplot(binned, aes(x = commontime(bin_datetime, tz = 'America/New_York'), #                    y = detectionrate_ins_buzz, color = flower)) + #   geom_line() + #   scale_x_datetime(labels = label_hour(tz = 'America/New_York')) + #   theme_buzzr(base_size = 14, mode = 'dark')

what: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
text: Returns time day either proportion 24-hour day (0 = midnight, 0.5 = noon, 1 = next midnight) decimal hour (0 = midnight, 6.5 = 6:30 , 23.5 = 11:30 PM). Seconds included calculation.

title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
code: time_of_day(times, time_format = "proportion")

title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
text: times POSIXct vector. time_format One 'proportion' (default) 'hour'.

title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
text: numeric vector length times.

title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
text: useful statistical analyses — example, fitting circular model daily activity finding hour peak buzz detections. plotting, use commontime label_hour instead.

title: Convert a date-time to a numeric time of day. — time_of_day
code: times <- as.POSIXct(   c('2023-08-09 00:00:00', '2023-08-09 06:00:00', '2023-08-09 18:30:00'),   tz = 'America/New_York' )  # Midnight = 0, 6 AM = 0.25, noon = 0.5 time_of_day(times) #> [1] 0.0000000 0.2500000 0.7708333  # As decimal hours: midnight = 0, 6 AM = 6, 6:30 PM = 18.5 time_of_day(times, time_format = 'hour') #> [1]  0.0  6.0 18.5

what: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
title: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
text: Applies trim_results every buzzdetect result file found recursively dir_results, saving trimmed file dir_trim preserving original directory structure.

title: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
code: trim_directory(   dir_results,   dir_trim,   activation_digits,   neurons_keep = NULL,   output_format = "rds",   if_exists = "stop",   workers = 1 )

title: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
text: dir_results Path directory containing buzzdetect result files. dir_trim Path output directory. Created automatically exist. activation_digits Integer. Number decimal places round activation values . neurons_keep Character vector neuron names retain (see trim_results). NULL (default), neurons kept. output_format Output file format. One "rds" (default) "csv". CSV files written without row names. if_exists output file already exists. One "stop" (default, throws error), "skip" (silently skips existing files), "overwrite" (overwrites warning). workers Number parallel workers. Defaults 1 (sequential). Parallelism uses parallel::mcmapply may supported platforms.

title: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
text: Invisibly returns character vector output file paths.

title: Trim activation columns for all result files in a directory. — trim_directory
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ dir_in  <- system.file('extdata/five_flowers', package = 'buzzr') dir_out <- file.path(tempdir(), 'five_flowers_trimmed')  # Trim all files, rounding to 2 decimal places, saved as .rds trim_directory(dir_in, dir_out, activation_digits = 2)  # Save trimmed files as CSV instead trim_directory(dir_in, dir_out, activation_digits = 2, output_format = 'csv')  # Re-run, keeping only the ins_buzz neuron and overwriting existing files trim_directory(   dir_in, dir_out,   activation_digits = 2,   neurons_keep = 'ins_buzz',   if_exists = 'overwrite' ) } # }

what: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
title: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
text: Rounds activation_ columns specified number decimal places. Optionally retains neurons named neurons_keep, dropping others. useful reducing file size archiving sharing results. original data frame modified.

title: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
code: trim_results(results, activation_digits, neurons_keep = NULL)

title: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
text: results data frame data.table activation_ columns. activation_digits Integer. Number decimal places round activation values . neurons_keep Character vector neuron names retain. Names may include omit activation_ prefix (e.g. "ins_buzz" "activation_ins_buzz" equivalent). activation columns dropped. NULL (default), neurons kept.

title: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
text: data.table rounded (optionally filtered) activation columns.

title: Trim activation columns in a results data frame. — trim_results
